examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First-In-First-Out page replacement algorithm for virtual memory simulation
examples.code:
def fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames):
    page_faults = 0
    memory = []
    
    for page in pages:
        if page not in memory:
            page_faults += 1
            if len(memory) >= frames:
                memory.pop(0)  # Remove oldest page (FIFO)
            memory.append(page)
        print(f"Page {page}: {'Fault' if page not in memory[:len(memory)-1] else 'Hit'} - Memory: {memory}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example: Reference string 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
pages = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
frames = 3
print(f"Page Faults with {frames} frames: {fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames)}")
examples.explanation: Simulates FIFO page replacement by maintaining a queue of pages in memory. When a page fault occurs and memory is full, the oldest page (first in) is evicted. Demonstrates Belady's anomaly when increasing frames can increase faults.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for storing frames in memory

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Least Recently Used page replacement using a counter-based approach
examples.explanation: LRU keeps track of when each page was last used with a counter. On page fault, the page with the smallest counter (least recently used) is evicted. More accurate than FIFO but requires additional hardware support.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) for scanning for LRU page
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for page table with counters

examples.title: TLB Lookup Simulation
examples.description: Simulates Translation Lookaside Buffer with associative lookup for fast address translation
examples.explanation: Simulates TLB behavior with associative lookup. On a TLB hit, physical address is generated in one memory access. On miss, page table is consulted and TLB is updated. Demonstrates how TLB improves effective memory access time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(TLB_SIZE) for associative search (hardware is parallel O(1))

examples.title: Clock (Second Chance) Page Replacement
examples.description: Implementation of Clock algorithm using reference bits for efficient page eviction
examples.code:
def clock_page_replacement(pages, frames):
    page_faults = 0
    
    # Initialize clock with (page, reference_bit)
    clock_hand = 0
    memory = []
    
    for page in pages:
        # Check if page is already in memory
        found = False
        for i, (p, ref) in enumerate(memory):
            if p == page:
                memory[i] = (page, 1)  # Set reference bit
                found = True
                break
        
        if not found:
            page_faults += 1
            
            # If memory is full, use clock algorithm
            while len(memory) >= frames:
                if memory[clock_hand][1] == 0:
                    # Replace this page
                    memory[clock_hand] = (page, 1)
                    clock_hand = (clock_hand + 1) % frames
                    break
                else:
                    # Give second chance, clear bit
                    memory[clock_hand] = (memory[clock_hand][0], 0)
                    clock_hand = (clock_hand + 1) % frames
            else:
                # Memory not full, just add
                memory.append((page, 1))
        
        print(f"Page {page}: {'Fault' if not found else 'Hit'}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example reference string
pages = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(f"Clock Page Faults: {clock_page_replacement(pages, 3)}")
examples.explanation: Clock algorithm uses a circular buffer with reference bits. Scans pages in circular fashion; if reference bit is 1, clears it and gives page second chance; if 0, evicts that page. Provides approximation of LRU with lower overhead.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) in worst case (multiple scans)
